answer: A user would pay for both 5-minute and 1-hour cache writes in the same request when cache entries with longer TTL (1-hour) must appear before shorter TTLs (5-minute), and there are cache misses for both. The token count is split between them based on three billing locations in the prompt: Position A (the token count at the highest cache hit), Position B (the token count at the highest 1-hour cache control block after A), and Position C (the token count at the last cache control block). The user is charged for cache read tokens for A, 1-hour cache write tokens for (B - A), and 5-minute cache write tokens for (C - B).

On the third turn, the lookback window of 20 blocks will not find a matching cache entry from the first turn because the breakpoint has moved 30 blocks forward (15 blocks per turn x 2 turns), which exceeds the 20-block lookback window, resulting in no cache hit.

✓ indicates that the cache remains valid, while ✘ indicates that the cache is invalidated. Enabling/disabling web search modifies the system prompt, so the system cache and messages cache are invalidated (✘), but the tools cache remains valid (✓).

cache_read_input_tokens: 8000
cache_creation_input_tokens: 1000
